report_metadata.report_title: CISA Shares Lessons Learned from an Incident Response Engagement

tools.name: Burp Suite Burp Scanner
tools.evidence_snippet: CISA detected this scanning activity by analyzing web logs and identifying signatures associated with the tool.

tools.name: RingQ
tools.evidence_snippet: The cyber threat actors staged RingQ, a publicly available defense evasion tool, for potential use.

tools.name: Stowaway
tools.evidence_snippet: The cyber threat actors used Stowaway, a publicly available multi-level proxy tool, to establish C2.

tools.name: fscan
tools.evidence_snippet: CISA identified the use of the fscan tool by analyzing evidence of its output found on disk.

cves.evidence_snippet: The cyber threat actors exploited CVE-2024-36401 for initial access on two GeoServers.

ttps.evidence_snippet: The cyber threat actors performed active scanning to identify vulnerabilities they could use for initial access.
The cyber threat actors gained remote access to the victim’s network using a desktop behind a virtual private server (VPS).
The cyber threat actors exploited CVE 2024-36401 on two of the organization’s public-facing GeoServers.
The cyber threat actors used PowerShell to download a payload.
The cyber threat actors employed indirect command execution via web shells.
The cyber threat actors abused BITS jobs.
The cyber threat actors established persistence through cron jobs.
The cyber threat actors uploaded web shells for persistence.
The cyber threat actors used valid accounts for persistence.
The cyber threat actors attempted to exploit CVE-2016-5195 to escalate privileges.
The cyber threat actors used brute force techniques to obtain login credentials for web services.
The cyber threat actors used cat /etc/passwd to discover local users.
The cyber threat actors used dir c:\, dir d:\, dir e:\, and type c:\ commands to identify files and directories on the SQL server.
The cyber threat actors used fscan to identify SSH listeners and FTP servers.
The cyber threat actors used tasklist on the SQL server.
The cyber threat actors performed ping sweeps of hosts within specific subnets.
The cyber threat actors used cat /etc/redhat-release and cat /etc/os-release commands to get Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) and Linux operating system information.
The cyber threat actors used ipconfig to check GeoServer 1’s and the SQL server’s network configurations.
The cyber threat actors executed commands such as netstat to obtain a listing of network connections to or from the systems they compromised.
The cyber threat actors used whoami on the SQL server.
The cyber threat actors used PowerShell and bitsadmin getfile to download payloads.
The cyber threat actors used a connection proxy to direct traffic from their C2 server.

summary: CISA shares lessons learned from an incident response engagement at a U.S. federal civilian executive branch (FCEB) agency, highlighting the importance of prompt patching and preparing for incidents by practicing incident response plans and implementing logging and aggregating logs in a centralized out-of-band location.
